title: SEGMENT 11 — Luxturna — the first US AAV approval and what it proved

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Luxturna — the first US AAV approval and what it proved
So now, finally, having walked through the framework, let me tell you the stories of the gene therapies that have made it through.
I am going to give you five, in chronological order: Luxturna, Zolgensma, Hemgenix, Casgevy, and Lyfgenia.
Each one is a regulatory milestone as much as a scientific one.
Each one taught the field something about what was possible.
We start with Luxturna.
December 19, 2017.
Voretigene neparvovec.
The first AAV gene therapy approved in the United States.
A turning point.
Luxturna treats a rare form of inherited blindness called Leber congenital amaurosis caused by mutations in the gene RPE65.
RPE65 encodes an enzyme essential for the visual cycle — the biochemical process by which the retina converts light into electrical signals.
Patients with two broken copies of RPE65 are born with severe vision impairment that progresses over time to complete blindness.
The patient population is small — a few thousand worldwide — and before Luxturna there was no treatment.
The product itself is an AAV2-based vector carrying a healthy copy of RPE65, injected directly under the retina in each eye in a separate surgical procedure.
The total amount of vector administered per eye is small.
The injection delivers the healthy gene to the retinal pigment epithelial cells where RPE65 normally functions.
The cells, having received a functional copy of the gene, start producing the enzyme.
The visual cycle resumes.
Patients gain functional vision.

The clinical trial that supported Luxturna's approval was a Phase 3 randomized controlled trial in 31 patients, with the endpoint being a multi-luminance mobility test — essentially, the ability of patients to navigate a standardized obstacle course under low-light conditions.
The treated patients showed dramatic improvements; the untreated control group did not.
The effect size was big enough, the safety profile was clean enough, and the patient population was small enough that the trial was sufficient as a pivotal study.
But the regulatory significance of Luxturna was not just that it worked.
It was that it proved several things to FDA, to the field, and to the broader medical community.
First, it proved that an AAV gene therapy could navigate the full regulatory pathway and emerge as an approved product in the United States.
Until December 2017, that had not been done.
There had been earlier approved gene therapies elsewhere — Glybera was approved in Europe in 2012 but commercially failed and was withdrawn — but the US had been a holdout.
Luxturna broke the seal.
Second, it proved that a Phase 3 trial in 31 patients could be sufficient for approval, provided the disease was rare enough, the effect size was big enough, and the endpoints were well-chosen.
This established a template that future small-population gene therapies would build on.
Third, it proved that the price would be high.
Luxturna launched at approximately $425,000 per eye, or $850,000 per patient for the bilateral treatment.
The pricing provoked controversy.

But it also established that the gene therapy market could sustain very high per-patient prices for transformative one-time treatments — which, in turn, created the financial conditions for the rest of the field to invest in gene therapy development.
Let me give you the analogy.
Luxturna was the first US AAV approval — for RPE65 retinal degeneration.
It gave the field the first proof that AAV gene therapy could be a real drug.
The molecular framework had been validated in academic papers for years.
The clinical safety had been demonstrated in early-phase trials.

But there is a difference between "demonstrated in trials" and "approved by FDA, with a label, with a commercial product, with insurance reimbursement, with patients getting it in a regular clinical setting.
" Luxturna closed that gap for the first time.
After Luxturna, every other AAV gene therapy program had a regulatory precedent to point to.
The conversation with the agency was no longer "is this kind of product approvable in principle?
" It was "given that AAV gene therapies are approvable, what specifically do you want to see for our product?

That shift in the default assumption — from theoretical possibility to demonstrated reality — was, I would argue, more important to the trajectory of the field than the specific clinical benefit Luxturna provided to its specific patient population, important as that was.
